generations.text:
**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it finds you far from the sea, and perhaps far from the heart. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though time has a way of eroding even the strongest of ties, I find myself writing to you now, not out of duty or obligation, but because the wind howls through these cliffs like a mourning wail, and I fear I may not see the morning.

The storm is upon us. The sky has turned a shade of iron, and the sea, which has always been my companion, now roars like a beast roused from its slumber. I’ve been keeping the light burning all night, as I always do, but tonight it feels heavier, as though the very air is thick with the weight of unspoken words. I’ve not slept, not since the wind began to rise, and I fear I may not wake again.

You remember the night we quarreled, the night I told you I would not be your father, and you said I was not your father to begin with. I have not forgotten that. I have not forgotten the look in your eyes when you left, or the silence that followed. I have not forgotten the way the sea seemed to mourn with me that night, as though it too had lost something.

But I have not forgotten the way you looked when you were a child, standing on the shore, eyes

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may come too late. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and the world has turned in ways I scarcely understand. I write to you now from the cliffs of St. Agnes, where the sea howls like a wounded beast and the wind bites through my bones. The lighthouse stands, though it is but a shell of what it once was. I have kept it lit through every storm, as I have always done, but tonight—tonight is different.

A terrible storm is coming, and I do not know if I will survive it. The waves grow wilder, the sky splits with lightning, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the world whole. I have seen such storms before, but never one that feels so... final.

You were always the child who questioned everything, my Eleanor. You asked why the lighthouse must stand, why we must endure the cold, why the sea must be feared. I told you the light is a promise, a beacon for those lost at sea. But you saw it as a prison, a chain that bound us to this lonely place. You left, as I had feared you would, with your questions and your doubts, and I was left with my silence.

I do not know if you still live in London, or if you have found your answers elsewhere. But I write to you now, not as a father

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

If you are reading these words, then I have either survived the night—or, by the grace of God, I have not. Either way, I write them with the weight of a man who has long since ceased to be the father you once knew, and yet, in the quiet of this lighthouse, with the howl of the wind upon the rocks and the sea rising like a beast in fury, I find myself compelled to speak.

Five years have passed since the last time I laid eyes upon you. Five years of silence, of letters left unanswered, of your letters unanswered. I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. I have not forgotten the day you left, nor the way you turned your back to the sea, as if it were a mirror of your own heart.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years now, and in this lonely place, I have come to understand the weight of solitude. The sea is a cruel mistress, and tonight, it is not the sea alone that threatens us. The storm is coming, and the winds are not kind. I have seen the sky darken with a fury that has not been seen in decades. The waves rise like the wrath of some forgotten god, and the lighthouse—my home, my duty, my only tether to the world—feels as if it may not hold.

I have always believed that a man must be strong, that a man must stand firm in the face of

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years, my child, and not a word from you. Not a letter, not a visit, not even a single word. I have long since ceased to count the days, for time has lost its meaning in this lighthouse, where the sea roars louder than any voice, and the wind sings songs of forgotten things.

You were but a girl when you left, and now you are grown, and I—well, I remain here, in this tower, where the world seems to have forgotten me. I have watched the stars for decades, and yet, none have ever shown me the path to your heart.

I have tried to forgive you, Eleanor. I have tried to believe that the silence between us was but a storm that would pass. But the storm has only grown, and I fear it will never cease.

Tonight, a terrible storm is coming. The sea is restless, and the sky is dark with clouds that have not seen the sun in days. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and I have made the preparations. But I do not know if I shall see dawn again.

If I do not, I ask you to remember this: I have never stopped loving you. Not once. I have raised you, and though you ran away, I did not chase you. I did

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast in the night, and the sea, that old adversary, rising in fury. The lighthouse stands firm, though I fear for its fate. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as the storm gathers, I find myself compelled to write.

You once called me a "heartless brute," and I suppose, in my stubbornness, I have earned that name. I have watched the world pass me by, content in my solitude, while you grew into a woman of spirit and sorrow. I have been a fool, Eleanor, a man who let pride and silence keep me from you. I have not sought you out, not even when the world around us changed.

But tonight, as the heavens weep and the waves claw at the rocks, I find myself thinking of you. I have spent these years in the company of the sea, its ceaseless rhythm a balm to my soul, yet it is not enough. I have not seen your face in a decade, and yet, in the dark, I find myself remembering your laughter, the way your eyes would light up when you spoke of the stars, and how you once called me "a man of stone and shadow."

I have not written to you, not even when your letters came, unopened, their ink faded by the time I found them. I have let the years pass, and

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit here by the window, the sea howling like a wounded beast, and the wind rattles the shutters as if it would tear this old house apart. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take me with it. I've spent the last five years in silence, but tonight, with the world turning upside down, I feel the need to speak. Not to the sea, but to you.

Five years ago, I sent you away. I told you I could not give you the life you deserved, that I was too old, too broken, too much of a failure. I told you I would rather see you die than live with the weight of my own sins. And you left, as I knew you would. You were always the brave one, Eleanor, the one who would not let the world break you. I was the one who would let it.

But now, as I watch the storm gather strength, I find myself thinking of you. I remember the nights you used to come to the lighthouse, when you were a child, and we would sit by the fire, talking of dreams and futures. You were full of life, full of hope, and I... I was just a man who had lost his way long before you were born.

I have no words to explain why I sent you away, no words that could undo the pain I've caused. But I do have this: I have not forgotten

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you with trembling hands, the ink smudged by the salt of the sea and the tears that have long been buried. Five years, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years since you left this house, this shore, this life that once was yours. I have not spoken your name in all that time, not because I have forgotten it, but because the weight of it has grown too heavy to bear.

The lighthouse stands tall and proud, as it always has, its beacon shining through the endless night. But tonight, my girl, the night is different. The wind howls like a wounded beast, the waves crash against the rocks with a fury I have never seen. The sea, which has always been my companion, now roars with a voice that feels like a warning.

I do not know if I will see dawn. I have heard the old sailors speak of the *Storm of the Black Maw*, a tempest that comes once in a lifetime, and takes all who do not heed its call. I have no illusions, Eleanor. If this storm breaks the lighthouse, if the sea swallows the cliffs, then I may not be here to greet the morning. And if I am not, I would have you know that I did not leave you in anger, nor in regret, but in sorrow.

You remember the night you left? The night the wind howled and the sea cried, and I stood at

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you this evening, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very bones of the earth. The lighthouse stands tall, though, and the light burns—steady, unwavering, as it always has. I have tended it for over thirty years, and though the years have worn me thin, the light remains. So, too, do I, though I fear not for long.

It has been five years since you left, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years. I have tried to forget you, as one forgets a dream upon waking. But the dream lingers. I have seen your face in the mist, in the flicker of the flame, in the silence between the waves. I have heard your name in the wind, in the cry of the gulls, in the hush of the night.

I do not know if you still remember me. I do not know if you remember the way I used to sing to you as a child, or the way I would hold your hand when the storms were worst. I do not know if you remember the stories I told you of the sea, of the stars, of the great, unchanging light that guides the lost and the weary home.

But I write now, not for your return, but for my own peace. For the knowledge that I have not forgotten, and that I have not given up.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse, and the sea, that old, unrelenting companion, growling in its sleepless rage. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may claim me before the dawn. But I must say these things before the silence of the grave swallows my voice.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five years of watching the horizon, of hearing the waves, of keeping the light burning in the tower, and of hearing your name whispered in the wind. I have not spoken to you, not in words, not in letters, not even in the silence between us. But I have not forgotten you, nor have I ceased to think of you.

You were a child once, and I was a man who believed that the sea could be tamed, that the world was a place of order and reason. But the world, like the sea, is wild, and I have learned that some things cannot be controlled. You chose your path, and I chose mine. We both walked away, and neither of us looked back.

But tonight, as the storm gathers, I find myself thinking of you more than ever. I have spent these years in the solitude of the lighthouse, with only the light and the wind for company. And in that solitude, I have come to see that I was wrong to let you go. I was

**[Sealed with a wax stamp bearing the crest of the Lighthouse at Skerry Point]**

**To: My Dearest Eliza**

**From: Your Loving Father, A. W. Hargrave**

**Date: April 12th, 1887**

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you now, as the wind howls through the stones and the sea roars like a beast in pain. The sky is a sickly green, and the waves rise like the wrath of some ancient god. I've seen many storms in my time, but this one... this one feels different. It feels like the end.

I've not seen you in five years, Eliza. Five long, lonely years. I've tried to write, but the letters always ended up in the sea, torn by the wind or lost in the fog. I've tried to call, but the sea is too loud, and the telephone—ah, the telephone! I've heard of it, but I can't bring myself to trust it. It's too much like the world that took you from me.

You were always a headstrong girl, Eliza. Too clever for your own good, too quick to take offense. I remember the day you left. You were seventeen, and I was too proud to ask you to stay. I thought you'd come back of your own accord, that you'd see the truth of things. But you didn

My Dearest Eliza,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not. I have been meaning to write to you for some time now, but the sea has always been a better companion than pen and paper. Yet tonight, as the wind howls like a banshee and the waves claw at the cliffs with a vengeance, I find myself with no choice but to put these words to parchment.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I wonder if you still remember me at all. I know you have your own life to live, your own children to raise, your own heart to mend. I should not presume to intrude upon that. But I must say this: I have never stopped loving you, Eliza. Not once.

You were always so quick to judge me, to think I was too stern, too distant. But I was only trying to keep us safe. You were young then, and I feared the world would take you too soon. I did not want to be the one to say goodbye. I did not want to be the one who failed you.

I have always been a lighthouse keeper, you know. Not just of the sea, but of your heart. I watched over you, even when you did not see it. I lit the beacon every night, not just to guide the ships, but to remind you that you were never alone. I used to think that if I could just keep the light burning, I

My Dearest Eliza,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may be a long while before I can be certain of your welfare. It has been five years since our last correspondence, and I must confess that I have often found myself longing for the sound of your voice and the sight of your familiar face. Though the sea has been my constant companion these many years, I find myself missing the warmth of family more than ever.

The weather has been most unkind of late, with the winds howling like the very devil and the sea churning with a fury that I have not seen since my early days as a young keeper. Tonight, however, the storm is set to come with a vengeance, and I find myself both anxious and uncertain of what the night may bring. The lighthouse stands strong, I trust, but I fear that even the sturdiest of structures may not withstand the wrath of such a tempest.

I have not spoken to you in so long, and I wonder if you still remember the days when we used to gather by the hearth and share stories of our childhood. I often find myself reminiscing about those times, when the world seemed so much simpler and the worries of the world were not so heavy upon my shoulders. I pray that you are not burdened by the same cares that have come to weigh upon my heart.

I must ask, my dear Eliza, if you have found peace in your life? Have you

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a beast at the door, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. I have not seen you in five years, and I do not know if I shall see you again. But tonight, I must speak, for the storm is coming, and I fear it may take me before I can say what I must.

You remember the day you left, the way you walked away from the door without looking back, as though you had no choice. I did not stop you, though I would have given my life to hold you. You were a child then, and I was a fool, thinking that love could be held in the same way as the light in the tower—steady, constant, unyielding. But love is not like a lamp, Eliza. It is like the sea: it comes, it goes, and sometimes, it does not return.

I have watched the waves for years, and in them, I have seen your face. I have seen your laughter, your anger, your silence. I have not written, not because I have no words, but because I feared what they might say. I feared they would be too much for you to bear, or too little. I feared that I would be remembered as the man who never understood, or who never let you go.

But I have let you go.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and yet the silence between us has grown heavier with each passing day. I have not written to you, nor have you written to me, and I wonder if you still remember who I am, or if time has blurred the edges of our past.

The lighthouse stands strong, as it always has, though the sea has grown more restless of late. The storms have grown fiercer, and I find myself watching the horizon with more than just duty. I have seen the waves rise higher, and I have felt the wind howl like a beast in pain. Tonight, a terrible storm is coming, and I am not sure I will survive it. I have lit the lamp, but I do not know if it will be enough.

I have thought of you often, Eliza. I have thought of the days we shared, the laughter, the arguments, the quiet moments when we both knew we were not quite the same. I have thought of the way you left, and how I have tried to forgive you, though I do not know if I have. I have tried to be the father you once needed, but I fear I have become something else—something I do't recognize.

The sea is cruel, and so is the world. I have no regrets, only questions. Why did you leave? Why did you not

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write these words with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since I last laid eyes upon you, and yet it feels as though the years have only grown heavier with each passing day. I have not spoken to you, nor have I sent a letter, for I feared the words might not be enough, and the silence might be better than the truth.

But tonight, as I stand upon the rocky cliffs of this desolate shore, the wind howling like the cries of the damned, I am forced to reckon with the truth I have long buried deep within my soul. A storm is coming—great, terrible, and unrelenting. The sea, which has long been my companion, now roars with fury, and I fear that I may not see the dawn.

I have spent these years in solitude, tending to the light, watching the horizon, and keeping the darkness at bay. It was a life of duty, of course, but also of regret. I have not been the father you deserved, nor the man you once believed in. I have been a shadow, a figure of stone, and you, my dear Eleanor, have become a memory I have not dared to call my own.

But now, as the storm gathers strength and the waves rise higher than ever before, I am forced to confess what I have long kept hidden. I have not written to you because I feared what I might say

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is now past midnight, and the wind howls like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old lighthouse. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, though tonight the sea is restless and the sky is black as a thief’s hand. The storm is coming, and I fear it may be my last night on this earth. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear I may never speak to you again. But I must write this, for if I do not, I fear my voice will be lost to the sea, and my words will be swallowed by the silence between us.

You were once my greatest pride, Eleanor. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes were full of fire and your hands were clenched like fists. You told me I was too old, too rigid, too much of a fool to understand the world you were trying to build. I did not argue. I did not beg. I simply nodded, and let you go. I thought, perhaps, that time would mend what time had torn. But time has not mended. It has only made the wound deeper.

I have not forgotten the way you looked when you left—your face pale, your hair wild, your heart full of something I could not name. You were not cruel, Eleanor. You were simply lost. And I, in my pride, let you

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles, not from the wind that howls at the window, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since you left, and though the sea has not spared me its fury, it has not spared me the ache of your absence either. I have kept the lighthouse, as you once told me I would, and though it is a lonely task, I have done it with pride. Yet now, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself questioning whether I have been a good father—or whether I have been a fool.

The sea, as ever, is a cruel mistress. It has taken much from me—my peace, my patience, and most of all, your love. I have tried to be the man you needed, but I fear I failed. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes held a fire that I could not match, and the way your voice, though filled with sorrow, still carried a promise I could not keep. I have not written to you, nor have I dared to hope for a reply. I have let the silence between us grow like the dunes that surround this rock, tall and unyielding, yet perhaps not as unbreakable as I once believed.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the weight of the storm pressing in from the sea, the wind howling like a banshee, and the waves crashing with a fury that seems to echo the pain I've carried in my heart. Five years have passed since I last saw you, and the silence between us has grown as thick as the fog that clings to these rocks each morning.

I've always been a man of routine, of duty, of the light that guides the ships through the darkest nights. But I've never been able to guide you back to me, not even in the way I've tried. You left, and I let you go, thinking it was for your own good. But now, as the storm threatens to swallow this lighthouse whole, I'm beginning to wonder if I've made a mistake.

I've watched the sea for years, and I've seen many storms. Some pass, some break the coast, and some... some take everything. I fear that this one might take me. And if it does, I want you to know that I never stopped loving you, that I've never stopped thinking of you, and that I've never stopped wishing I could have been the father you needed.

I've spent these years in solitude, but I've also spent them in regret. I've tried to be the man you deserve, but I've failed. I've tried to be the man I thought you needed, but I've

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that aches like the wind howling through the cracks in this old lighthouse. Five years, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years since you left us—left me, your father, and your mother, and the life we once shared. I have not spoken your name aloud in all that time, for fear it would bring back the tears and the silence that followed. But tonight, the storm is coming. A terrible, howling tempest that has already battered the cliffs and sent the sea roaring like a beast in pain. I fear I may not see dawn. And so, I must write.

You were always the light in our lives, Eleanor. The one who brought warmth to the cold stone walls of our home, who laughed in the rain and danced in the snow. But something changed, my dear. You grew distant. I do not know what made you leave, or why you chose to walk away from the only family you had. I tried to reach you, to understand. But you never answered. And so, I let you go, as a father must when his child walks into the dark.

But now, I find myself alone again. The lighthouse, once a place of safety for sailors, now stands as a monument to my own loneliness. The storms grow fiercer each year, and I fear this one will be the last. I have no one to call for help,

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you with the wind howling like the wails of a damned soul, the sea a beast of fury, and the lighthouse—my steadfast companion—shining through the storm, as it has always done. I have not written to you in five years, and I do not know if you will read these words, or if they will reach you at all. But I must try. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than the light from my lamp.

You were a child of fire, Eliza. Always burning bright, always in motion. I remember the day you first climbed the tower, with your little hands gripping the railings as if they were the only thing keeping you from falling into the sea. You were six, and I was a fool not to see the fire in your eyes. You were not meant to be caged, and I, in my stubbornness, tried to bind you.

I have not seen you since the day you left. I do not know if it was for love, or for something else. I have no idea if you still remember me. I have no idea if you remember the stories I told you of the sea, or if you think of me as the man who kept the light burning through the night, or if you think of me as the man who never understood you.

But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you. You

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit here by the fire, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the glass, and I find myself writing these words—words I’ve long kept buried beneath the weight of silence. Five years have passed since you last called me *Father*, and yet the ache of your absence has never dulled. It lingers like the salt on my skin, sharp and unrelenting.

I’ve always said the sea is a fickle mistress, but tonight, it feels as though she has turned her back on us all. The sky is a bruised canvas, the waves a relentless hammer, and I fear we may not see dawn. But I write this not in despair, but in the hope that if I should not make it through, these words might reach you.

You were a bright thing, Eleanor—brighter than the lantern I keep burning on this cliff. I watched you grow, your laughter echoing through the halls of our home, your curiosity as boundless as the horizon. But then came the day you left. I do not know what drove you, only that you chose to walk away, and I chose to let you. I told myself it was for your own good, but I know now that I was merely afraid to face the truth of what we had become.

You were always the one who saw beyond the stones and the tides. You asked questions that made me squirm, and I could not answer them. I

**Letter from a Grizzled Victorian-Era Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*October 12th, 1887*  
*The Lighthouse, North Point, Cornwall*

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in a state of disbelief. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have spent much of that time wondering if I should have written, or if the silence was the only thing that could have kept me from being a fool. But now, as the storm gathers over the cliffs and the wind howls like a pack of wolves, I find myself compelled to write. Perhaps it is the storm, or perhaps it is the weight of years pressing down upon me. Either way, I cannot let this night pass without trying to make amends.

You were sixteen when you left, Eleanor. I remember the day as if it were yesterday—your face pale, your eyes full of a fire I could not understand, and your words sharp as glass. "You never listen," you said. "You never see me." And I suppose you were right. I had been so busy keeping this lighthouse running, so consumed by the sea and its moods, that I failed to notice the storm brewing in your heart.

I have thought often of you, though I have not written. I've kept the lantern lit, not just for the ships, but for the memories that

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these ancient walls. The sea rages, and I fear the storm will claim us all. But I must say these words before the night takes me.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken your name in all that time. Yet tonight, as the heavens weep and the waves claw at the cliffs, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years. You were my light, my guiding star, and now the world seems to be darkening once more.

I have not forgotten the day you left, not the way you turned your back on me with that cold, unyielding gaze. You said I was too old, too set in my ways, that I could not understand the world you wished to live in. But I have not changed, Eliza. I still keep this lighthouse, still watch over the sea, still find purpose in the eternal dance of light and dark. I have not forgotten the promise I made to your mother, to keep the light burning for those who sail the waters.

You were a fire in my life, and I have tried to keep that flame alive in my heart, even when you walked away. I have not written to you, not because I did not care, but because I feared what I might say. I feared that my words would not be enough, that they would not reach you

I trust this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. It is with a heavy heart that I write these words, for I have not held you in my thoughts as often as I should have, and the silence between us has grown longer than the years we have not spoken. But tonight, as the storm brews on the horizon, I find myself compelled to reach out once more.

The sea has been restless these past few days, and the wind has grown colder, carrying with it the scent of salt and sorrow. I have watched the waves crash against the cliffs with a ferocity that seems almost... personal. And now, as the sky darkens and the clouds gather like a shroud, I am reminded of the storms of my youth, when I first took up the mantle of this lighthouse. Back then, I thought I was invincible, that the sea would never take me. But the sea is fickle, and tonight, it may take more than I can give.

I have been keeping the light burning, as I always have, though my hands tremble with the cold and my bones ache with the weight of the years. The light is a beacon, a promise, a reminder that even in the darkest of nights, there is still a way forward. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though the silence between us has grown thick as the fog that clings to these cliffs at dawn, I find myself compelled to write, as though the act alone might bridge the chasm that has formed between us.

The sea has been restless of late, and tonight, it is said the tempest will come with a fury that has not been seen in decades. The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the waves, they say, will rise like mountains. I have spent my life watching the horizon, but tonight, I fear I may not see the dawn.

I have not written to you because I did not know what words could be said. I have not spoken to you because I feared what might be heard. Yet now, as the storm gathers, I find myself thinking of you more than I ever have. I think of the days when we were children, when you would run up to the lighthouse with your little lamp in hand, and I would let you sit with me, watching the stars. You used to call me "Old Grizzle," and I would laugh, though I knew it was not a name I would keep long.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a beast unleashed, and the sea roars as though it would devour the very stones of this cliff. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I fear the silence between us has grown heavier than the fog that clings to these shores. You were once my pride, my light in the darkness—now I wonder if I have become a shadow to you.

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*April 2nd, 1897*  
*The Lighthouse, White崖 Point, Yorkshire*  
*Dear Miss Eleanor,*

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea has grown restless, as though it knows the storm is coming. I’ve been keeping the light burning all night, though I can’t say I’ve been able to sleep. The lantern’s beam cuts through the darkness like a blade, and I sometimes wonder if it's meant to guide ships or to remind me of what I’ve lost.

It’s been five years since we last spoke, and I’ve tried, in my own way, to be the kind of father you deserved. I’ve raised the flag each morning at dawn, I’ve kept the oil lamps trimmed, and I’ve watched the skies with more care than I ever gave your mother’s affairs. But I suppose you’ve heard nothing of that, nor of the many letters I’ve sent you, asking for a chance to talk, to explain, to be more than just a man who lives alone in a tower.

I’ve been thinking of you often, especially when the wind is high and the sea roars. You were always the one who could calm the storm in my heart, though I never said so. I remember the night you were born,

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these stone walls. The sea is restless tonight, and the storm is coming—great, black, and merciless. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear this may be the last letter I ever write. But I must say it, for the sake of what once was, and perhaps for the sake of what might yet be.

You were twelve when you left, and I remember the day like it was yesterday. You stood at the door, your eyes wide with a mixture of fear and defiance, and said, "I don’t want to be a lighthouse keeper’s daughter." I told you then that you would always be my daughter, no matter where you went. I told you I would never stop loving you. But you did not stay. You left with the wind, and I with it.

I have not seen you since, and I have not written. I told myself that time would mend what words could not, that distance would soften what silence could not. But the years have passed, and the silence remains. I have come to understand that love is not always enough, and that some wounds do not heal with time.

My Dearest Eleanor,

The lighthouse, my steadfast companion, stands firm against the elements, though I fear the storm that is upon us may be too great for even it to withstand. I have been keeping the light burning all night, for it is the duty of a lighthouse keeper to ensure that no ship is lost to the dark. But tonight, the sky is dark and heavy, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that shakes the very foundations of this lonely tower.
